hx_brief: 58yo male with treated hypertension presents to his PCP reporting 'my blood pressure medication must be working too well — I keep feeling lightheaded when I stand up.' He mentions his ankles have been swollen for 2 weeks.

hints.hx: Started amlodipine 6 months ago. Orthostatic lightheadedness on rising. Also notes his voice has become slightly hoarse over the past month and he occasionally has trouble swallowing large pills. No chest pain. No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional fatigue climbing stairs.
hints.pe: HR 78, BP sitting 142/88 / standing 138/86 — orthostasis not confirmed. JVP 3cm above sternal angle. Mild bilateral ankle edema. Cardiac: early diastolic murmur best heard at left sternal border in held expiration, leaning forward. Wide pulse pressure. Bounding radial pulses. PMI laterally displaced.
hints.img: Echo: Severe aortic regurgitation. LV end-diastolic dimension 68mm (dilated). EF 48% — declining. Regurgitant fraction 55%. Aortic root 4.8cm. Ascending aorta 5.1cm. CXR: Cardiomegaly. Prominent ascending aorta silhouette. No pulmonary vascular congestion yet.

lab_results.CMP: BNP 640 pg/mL. Creatinine 1.2. Otherwise normal.
lab_results.EKG: LVH with strain. Sinus tachycardia. Prolonged PR 220ms.
lab_results.Trop: 0.06 ng/mL — chronically mildly elevated from LV wall stress.
lab_results.Specialty: Cardiac MRI: Regurgitant volume 58mL per beat. Regurgitant fraction 54%. LV end-systolic volume index elevated — approaching surgical threshold. Ascending aorta 5.1cm — aneurysmal. LVEDP 22mmHg on subsequent cath. Surgical AVR + ascending aorta replacement indicated.

hx_brief: 44yo female with a 6-month history of fatigue and progressive dyspnea referred to pulmonology after failing 'asthma' treatment. She is a lifetime non-smoker who works in finance.

hints.hx: Exertional dyspnea started insidiously — she now gets winded climbing one flight. No wheeze. No cough. Spirometry at PCP showed restriction not obstruction — never followed up. She also mentions Raynaud's since her 20s and her fingers have felt 'puffy and stiff' in the mornings. Dry eyes. Dry mouth. Two spontaneous first-trimester miscarriages.
hints.pe: Subtle facial telangiectasias. Sclerodactyly — tight skin over fingers. Nailfold capillaroscopy: giant capillary loops and dropout areas. Cardiac: loud P2. Right parasternal heave. No peripheral edema yet. O2 sat 93% at rest, drops to 86% with 6-minute walk.
hints.img: HRCT Chest: Bilateral ground-glass opacities and reticulation in lower lobes — NSIP pattern of ILD. No honeycombing. Echo: RVSP estimated 52mmHg. RV dilation. Moderate TR. Right heart catheterization: mPAP 38mmHg, PCWP 10mmHg, PVR 7.2 Wood units — pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.EKG: Right axis deviation. R>S in V1. P pulmonale.
lab_results.ABG: pO2 68 on room air. A-a gradient 28.
lab_results.Specialty: PFTs: FVC 58% predicted. TLC 62% predicted. DLCO 42% predicted — severely reduced, out of proportion to restriction. ANA positive 1:640 nucleolar pattern. Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I): Positive. Anti-centromere: Negative. Consistent with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis with combined ILD and pulmonary arterial hypertension.

hx_brief: 67yo male with well-controlled type 2 diabetes and a history of 'prostate trouble' presents for his annual visit. His wife prompted him to come in because he seemed confused during a road trip last month.

hints.hx: He had one episode of confusion lasting about 2 hours while driving — resolved spontaneously. Also reports waking 3-4x nightly to urinate (attributed to BPH), intermittent constipation, and diffuse 'bone achiness' he attributes to arthritis. PCP prescribed vitamin D 6 months ago without checking levels. No prior kidney stones. Drinks plenty of water.
hints.pe: Alert and oriented. BP 148/90. Mild proximal muscle weakness — difficulty rising from chair. DTRs 1+ bilaterally with delayed relaxation phase. Mild right flank fullness. No neck masses palpable. Corneal band keratopathy visible on penlight exam at limbus.
hints.img: Renal ultrasound: Right kidney 8.4cm, left 9.1cm. Bilateral cortical echogenicity increased. Small bilateral renal calculi. No hydronephrosis. Bone scan: Diffuse increased uptake in axial skeleton — non-specific. Skeletal survey: Subperiosteal resorption radial aspect 2nd and 3rd phalanges.

lab_results.CMP: Calcium 12.4 mg/dL (elevated). Phosphorus 1.8 (low). Creatinine 1.7 (mildly elevated). Glucose 118. Chloride 108 (hyperchloremic).
lab_results.UA: 24hr urine calcium 520mg/day. No casts. Trace protein.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.34 — mild metabolic acidosis (hyperchloremic from PTH effect on bicarbonate).
lab_results.Specialty: Intact PTH: 388 pg/mL (severely elevated; normal 15-65). 25-OH Vitamin D: 18 ng/mL (deficient — not the cause; PTH remains elevated despite supplementation). Technetium-99m sestamibi: Focal retention inferior left parathyroid — adenoma. PTH-dependent hypercalcemia = primary hyperparathyroidism.

hx_brief: 48yo male alcoholic presents to the ED at 2am with severe chest and upper back pain immediately after a prolonged violent vomiting episode at a party. Triage note: 'rule out MI vs. pancreatitis.'

hints.hx: Drank heavily for 6 hours, then had 4-5 forceful vomiting episodes. The last episode was followed immediately by a tearing, searing pain across his chest and back — 'like something ripped.' He cannot find a position of comfort. No prior cardiac history. No gallstones. His friend says he 'went white and grabbed his chest right after he puked.'
hints.pe: BP 88/60, HR 128, RR 28, Temp 37.9C. Profuse diaphoresis. Subcutaneous crepitus palpable at the base of the neck bilaterally. Hamman's sign on auscultation — mediastinal crunch synchronous with heartbeat. Breath sounds absent at the left base. Abdomen: epigastric rigidity, no bowel sounds.
hints.img: CXR: Left pleural effusion, pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema tracking up the neck. Left hydropneumothorax. CT chest with contrast: Full-thickness distal esophageal tear, left posterolateral wall, 3.1cm defect. Oral contrast extravasating into the mediastinum. Bilateral pleural effusions — left greater than right. Pleural tap: amylase-rich fluid, pH 6.0.

lab_results.CMP: Na 131, Cr 1.6, BUN 38. Amylase 280 (salivary isoenzyme — NOT pancreatic).
lab_results.Trop: 0.04 — borderline, likely demand in setting of shock.
lab_results.Lipase: Normal — critical finding. Amylase elevation is salivary, not pancreatic.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.28, pCO2 32, pO2 68 on 6L NC. Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation.
lab_results.Coags: PT 16.2, INR 1.4 — early coagulopathy from sepsis.
lab_results.Specialty: Esophagogastroscopy (performed in OR): Full-thickness longitudinal tear at the gastroesophageal junction, left posterolateral, 3cm length. No active bleeding — mediastinal soilage confirmed. Surgical repair required emergently.

hx_brief: 17yo male presents to neurology after his third episode of sudden-onset severe headache, vomiting, and right-sided weakness that resolved over 2-3 days. His mother has similar episodes. MRI between attacks: normal. Diagnosed with 'complex migraine.'

hints.hx: Episodes since age 12. Each lasts 2-4 days: severe headache, nausea, vomiting, then right arm weakness and slurred speech — all resolving completely. Between attacks he is normal academically but his PE teacher says he tires faster than peers. Mother has migraines and was 'told her hearing loss is from the migraines.' Maternal uncle died at 34 from a 'stroke.' Patient has had progressive bilateral sensorineural hearing loss since age 14. Short stature noted. No drug use.
hints.pe: Height 5th percentile for age. Mildly ptotic left eyelid. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss on Weber/Rinne. Mild proximal limb weakness symmetric. No focal deficits currently. Fundoscopy: bilateral optic disc pallor subtle. Cognitive testing: normal baseline but slow processing.
hints.img: MRI brain (during episode): DWI-restricted lesion in left parieto-occipital cortex — does NOT follow vascular territory. T2/FLAIR: bilateral basal ganglia signal changes, white matter lesions. MR spectroscopy: elevated lactate peak at 1.33 ppm. MRA: normal vessels — no stenosis, no dissection, no aneurysm.

lab_results.CMP: Lactate 4.8 mmol/L at rest (markedly elevated). Pyruvate elevated. Lactate:pyruvate ratio >20.
lab_results.EKG: Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern — delta waves. PR interval short.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.31, bicarb 16 — mild metabolic acidosis from lactate accumulation.
lab_results.Specialty: Muscle biopsy: Ragged red fibers on Gomori trichrome stain. Subsarcolemmal mitochondrial accumulation. Electron microscopy: abnormal mitochondrial cristae. Genetic testing: m.3243A>G point mutation in MT-TL1 gene (mitochondrial tRNA-Leu). Heteroplasmy 68% in blood, 84% in muscle. Diagnosis: MELAS — Mitochondrial Encephalomyopathy, Lactic Acidosis, and Stroke-like episodes.

hx_brief: 54yo male presents to GI with 8 months of severe epigastric pain, profuse watery diarrhea, and 22-pound weight loss. H. pylori tested twice — negative both times. PPI therapy at double dose for 4 months: no improvement.

hints.hx: Pain is postprandial, burning, severe. Diarrhea is watery, up to 8 times daily — no blood, no mucus. Weight loss is dramatic — clothes no longer fit. He avoids eating because eating triggers pain. No alcohol. No NSAIDs. No family history of GI cancer. He had a 'stomach infection' 2 years ago treated with antibiotics — never felt right since. Hypoalbuminemia found at PCP visit last month.
hints.pe: Cachectic. BP 102/68, HR 98. Bilateral pitting edema to the knees — albumin-driven. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. Skin: no flushing, no rash.
hints.img: CT abdomen with contrast: Marked diffuse thickening of the gastric rugal folds, predominantly in the fundus and body — folds up to 3.2cm in width. No gastric outlet obstruction. No lymphadenopathy. No liver lesions. Upper endoscopy: Giant, tortuous rugal folds covering the entire fundus and body. Mucosa appears hyperemic, friable. Antrum: spared. Random biopsies taken throughout.

lab_results.CBC: WBC normal. Hgb 10.2 (normocytic — protein-losing). Eosinophilia absent.
lab_results.CMP: Albumin 1.8 g/dL (severe hypoalbuminemia). Total protein 3.9. Normal LFTs. Normal renal function.
lab_results.UA: No proteinuria — ruling out nephrotic syndrome as cause of hypoalbuminemia.
lab_results.Specialty: Full-thickness surgical biopsy (endoscopic biopsy insufficient — only samples superficial mucosa): Foveolar hyperplasia with marked pit elongation, gland atrophy, and cystic dilation of glands deep in the mucosa. Parietal cell loss (achlorhydria). Edematous lamina propria. No malignancy. No H. pylori. Alpha-1 antitrypsin fecal clearance markedly elevated — confirming protein-losing gastropathy. TGF-alpha overexpression on immunostaining. Diagnosis: Menetrier's Disease.

hx_brief: 29yo female presents to her primary care physician with 4 months of intermittent severe abdominal pain, joint pain, and 'dark urine' that comes and goes. She has had two ER visits with negative workups.

hints.hx: Episodes last 3-5 days, then completely resolve. Abdominal pain is severe but exam is always benign — previous CT and ultrasound both normal. Takes oral contraceptives. The last two episodes followed her taking diclofenac for joint pain. Joint pain is mild and migratory. Episodes associated with mild confusion and one episode of wrist drop that resolved. Family history: her maternal aunt had similar unexplained abdominal attacks.
hints.pe: Currently between episodes. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Mild proximal upper extremity weakness right > left. Mild peripheral sensory changes in a glove distribution. No jaundice. No rash. BP 158/98 (baseline unknown). Dark-colored urine in specimen cup — darkens further after standing 10 minutes under fluorescent light.
hints.img: CT Abdomen (current visit): No acute pathology — identical to prior two studies. MRI Brain: No lesions. EMG/NCS: Axonal motor neuropathy pattern, predominantly proximal upper extremities.

lab_results.CMP: Na 122 (hyponatremia — SIADH). Mild transaminase elevation AST 62, ALT 54. Otherwise normal.
lab_results.UA: Dipstick: 1+ blood (false positive — porphyrins). No RBCs on microscopy. Sample turns deep burgundy after 30min in light.
lab_results.Specialty: Urine porphobilinogen (PBG): 94 µmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <2). Urine delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA): Elevated 8x ULN. Fecal porphyrins: Normal — rules out variegate porphyria and hereditary coproporphyria. Erythrocyte PBG deaminase activity: 45% of normal — confirms porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency. Acute intermittent porphyria confirmed. OCP and NSAIDs are precipitants — must be stopped.

hx_brief: 19yo male college student presents to student health with 1 week of severe sore throat, fever, and left-sided neck swelling. Rapid strep: negative. Mono spot: negative. Started on azithromycin 5 days ago — worsening.

hints.hx: Sore throat started acutely, but the neck swelling appeared 4 days later and is progressively worsening — now he can barely turn his head. Fever 39.4C daily. He denies IV drug use. Sexually active. No dental work recently. The left side of his neck is indurated and exquisitely tender. He mentioned 'I feel like something is squeezing in my chest' — attributed to anxiety. New onset pleuritic right-sided chest pain today.
hints.pe: Toxic-appearing. Temp 39.6C, HR 118, BP 104/72. Oropharynx: peritonsillar fullness left > right, uvular deviation. Left anterior neck: indurated, exquisitely tender, 4cm fluctuant mass at the angle of the jaw extending toward the carotid sheath. Decreased breath sounds right base. Abdomen: mild RUQ tenderness.
hints.img: CT neck with contrast: Thrombosis of the left internal jugular vein with surrounding phlegmon from the oropharynx tracking down the carotid sheath. 4.2cm peripherally enhancing abscess at left parapharyngeal space. CT chest: Multiple bilateral peripheral wedge-shaped nodules — some cavitating. Right-sided empyema. Septic pulmonary emboli pattern.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 28,400 with 94% PMNs and toxic granulation. Hgb 11.8. Platelets 88 (sepsis-related).
lab_results.CMP: Tbili 3.2, AST 88, ALT 72 — Lemierre hepatitis. Albumin 2.4. Cr 1.8.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.32, pO2 74 on 4L — septic emboli causing V/Q mismatch.
lab_results.Specialty: Blood cultures x4: Fusobacterium necrophorum — gram-negative anaerobic rod. Sensitivity: penicillin-resistant strain, susceptible to metronidazole + beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor. IR-guided drainage of parapharyngeal abscess: foul-smelling purulent material. Abscess culture: Fusobacterium necrophorum confirmed.

hx_brief: 48yo male presented to orthopedics with 8 months of bilateral knee pain and 'difficulty walking on uneven ground.' His primary care attributed it to obesity and osteoarthritis.

hints.hx: Balance problems — falls twice in the past month. The knee pain is actually less bothersome than the gait difficulties. Denies back pain or radiating pain. Also has urinary urgency with occasional leakage — attributed to prostate issues by his urologist, who found only mild BPH. His wife says he 'shuffles and can't pick up his feet.' Had a subarachnoid hemorrhage 9 months ago — coiled, 'full recovery.'
hints.pe: Cognitive: MMSE 22/30 — impaired recall, executive function, orientation to date. Gait: magnetic — wide-based, shuffling, en-bloc turning, cannot lift feet off ground ('glued' appearance). No resting tremor. No rigidity. No bradykinesia. No Babinski. Grasp reflex present. Urinary urgency confirmed — normal prostate on DRE.
hints.img: MRI Brain: Markedly enlarged lateral and third ventricles disproportionate to sulcal atrophy — Evans' ratio 0.38 (abnormal >0.3). Periventricular FLAIR signal — transependymal CSF flow. Corpus callosum stretched and thinned. No cortical atrophy. SPECT: No amyloid uptake.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.UA: Normal — no infection. Post-void residual 180mL (elevated — neurogenic bladder).
lab_results.Specialty: Lumbar puncture: Opening pressure 18 cmH2O (normal-high). CSF: Protein 42mg/dL, glucose normal, cells 0 — no infection or malignancy. Large-volume tap (40mL removed): Gait reassessed 1 hour later — 10-meter walk time improved from 24s to 11s. MMSE improved 4 points. Urinary urgency improved. Tap test positive — VP shunt indicated. Prior SAH caused communicating hydrocephalus.

hx_brief: 34yo female presents to rheumatology after 14 months of joint swelling, a facial rash that appeared after a beach vacation, and two episodes of pleuritis.

hints.hx: Rash appears specifically on sun-exposed areas and lasts days. Two painless oral ulcers in the past year. Hair thinning diffusely — clumps on pillow. She had a miscarriage at 10 weeks last year. Noticed her legs swell and urine looks frothy when she's stressed. Started hydroxychloroquine empirically 2 months ago with mild improvement. Her PCP ran an 'autoimmune panel' — ANA was 'weakly positive' but nothing further was done.
hints.pe: Malar erythema — butterfly distribution, sparing nasolabial folds. Two small oral ulcers on buccal mucosa. Bilateral wrist and MCP synovitis. Basilar dullness on percussion bilaterally — small pleural effusions. BP 152/96 (previously normal). 2+ pitting edema bilateral ankles.
hints.img: CXR: Bilateral small pleural effusions. Echo: Trace pericardial effusion. Renal ultrasound: Mildly echogenic kidneys bilaterally — nephropathy pattern. MRI Kidneys: Bilateral cortical thinning.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 2.6 (leukopenia). Hgb 9.2 (normocytic). Platelets 82,000 (thrombocytopenia). Direct Coombs: Positive.
lab_results.CMP: Creatinine 2.1 (up from 0.8 one year ago). BUN 34. Albumin 2.6.
lab_results.UA: 3+ protein. RBC casts on microscopy — nephritis. 24hr urine protein: 3.8g. Dysmorphic RBCs.
lab_results.Coags: aPTT prolonged 58s. Lupus anticoagulant: Positive. Anti-cardiolipin IgG: Elevated.
lab_results.Specialty: Renal biopsy: Class IV diffuse proliferative lupus nephritis — subendothelial immune deposits, 'full house' immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q). Anti-dsDNA: 840 IU/mL. Anti-Smith: Positive. C3 38 (low). C4 4 (low). SLEDAI 22. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome concurrent — explains miscarriage.

hx_brief: 66yo male with a history of GERD presents to his gastroenterologist for a surveillance endoscopy. He mentions 'I feel great — just here for the routine scope.'

hints.hx: 25 years of GERD, on PPI daily. Scope 3 years ago showed Barrett's esophagus — low grade dysplasia at that time. Lost 8 pounds over the past 6 months, which he attributes to 'finally dieting.' Noticed occasional difficulty swallowing large pills over the past 4 months. No odynophagia. No hematemesis. 30 pack-year smoking history, quit 12 years ago. Drinks 2 glasses of wine nightly.
hints.pe: BMI 23 (was 27 three years ago). No palpable lymphadenopathy. No Virchow's node. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation. No hepatomegaly. No ascites.
hints.img: EGD: At the gastroesophageal junction, an irregular raised mucosal lesion 2.8cm with surface nodularity and contact bleeding — not present on prior scope. Biopsies taken from 8 quadrants. CT Chest/Abdomen: Wall thickening at GEJ. Two enlarged regional lymph nodes 1.2-1.5cm. No distant metastases.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 10.8 — mild normocytic anemia (occult blood loss).
lab_results.CMP: Albumin 3.1. CEA 8.4 (mildly elevated). CA19-9 normal. Otherwise normal.
lab_results.Specialty: Biopsy results: Invasive adenocarcinoma — moderately differentiated. HER2/neu: Positive (3+) by IHC — eligible for trastuzumab. PD-L1 CPS score: 12. Arising from Barrett's metaplasia. Staging: cT2N1M0 (Stage IIB). Endoscopic resection not appropriate — requires definitive chemoradiation followed by surgery. Note: Adenocarcinoma of lower esophagus/GEJ — distinct from mid-esophageal squamous cell carcinoma.

hx_brief: 48yo female referred to neurology for 2 years of progressive gait instability, multiple unexplained falls, and episodes of painful muscle spasms in her back so severe she was hospitalized twice for 'back pain crisis.' Spine MRI: normal.

hints.hx: Falls occur without warning — not vertigo, not presyncope. She describes her legs as 'boards' — stiff, rigid, difficult to initiate movement. The back spasms are triggered by sudden noise, touch, or emotional distress — during one episode her back arched so severely she fractured a lumbar vertebra. She has type 1 diabetes and autoimmune thyroiditis. Diazepam given during a hospitalization gave dramatic relief — attributed to 'muscle relaxant effect.' She is wheelchair-bound now.
hints.pe: Continuous muscle rigidity of the paraspinal muscles and abdomen — board-like abdominal wall even at rest. Exaggerated startle response — touching her shoulder causes a full-body spasm. Gait: stiff, wide-based, robotic. Hyperreflexia throughout. No upper motor neuron signs on imaging. No cranial nerve deficits. No cerebellar signs.
hints.img: MRI brain and spine: Normal. EMG: Continuous motor unit activity at rest in paraspinal and limb muscles — does not suppress with voluntary relaxation or sleep. No denervation potentials. No myopathic features. Nerve conduction: normal.

lab_results.CMP: Glucose 214 (DM1). HbA1c 9.1. Otherwise normal.
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-GAD65 antibodies: 4,200 IU/mL (markedly elevated — normal <1.0). Anti-amphiphysin: negative. Anti-glycine receptor: negative. CSF analysis: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated IgG index, oligoclonal bands — intrathecal inflammation. Thyroid peroxidase antibodies elevated. GAD65 antibody level correlates with disease severity. Diagnosis: Stiff Person Syndrome (SPS) — autoimmune, GAD65-positive variant.

hx_brief: 32yo male competitive marathon runner presents to sports medicine with 6 months of progressive exertional dyspnea and two episodes of syncope during races. His cardiologist said his ECG showed 'athlete's heart changes — nothing to worry about.'

hints.hx: Syncope occurred both times at peak exertion — mile 18 and mile 22 of separate marathons. No prodrome, no palpitations he noticed beforehand, just collapsed and woke up on the ground. His father died suddenly at 41 during a recreational soccer game — attributed to 'heart attack.' Paternal uncle also died young. No drug use. No cocaine. He is otherwise healthy, no symptoms at rest.
hints.pe: Athletic build, BMI 21. BP 118/74. HR 52 at rest. No murmur. No signs of HF. Mild right ventricular heave on palpation. Normal S1 S2. No S3 or S4.
hints.img: Echo: Right ventricle markedly dilated, RV wall motion abnormality — segmental dyskinesia of the RV free wall ('epsilon wave territory'). RVEF 32%. LV: normal size, normal EF 62%. No valvular disease. Cardiac MRI: Fatty infiltration of the RV free wall with fibrosis on late gadolinium enhancement in a non-ischemic subepicardial pattern. RV free wall thinning. 'Triangle of dysplasia' involvement: inlet, apex, infundibulum.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.EKG: Epsilon wave in V1-V3 — pathognomonic. T-wave inversions V1-V4. Prolonged S-wave upstroke (>55ms) in V1-V3. Frequent PVCs with left bundle branch morphology (RV origin).
lab_results.Specialty: Electrophysiology study: Inducible sustained ventricular tachycardia with LBBB morphology and superior axis — confirming RV outflow tract origin. Genetic testing: PKP2 (plakophilin-2) pathogenic variant — desmosomal protein mutation. Autosomal dominant. First-degree relatives require screening. ICD implanted. Diagnosis: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC).

hx_brief: 76yo female with a history of recurrent urinary tract infections and type 2 diabetes presents with 2 days of confusion, decreased urine output, and mild flank discomfort.

hints.hx: Lives alone — her neighbor called 911. Has been treated for 'recurrent UTIs' — 3 antibiotic courses in 6 months, but urine cultures were rarely sent. Recently started oxybutynin for urinary urgency. Takes lisinopril, metformin, and furosemide. Hasn't been eating or drinking well for 4 days. Bladder feels full but she 'can't go.'
hints.pe: Temperature 38.8C. BP 78/48. HR 124. GCS 11 — confused. Suprapubic dullness and tenderness — distended bladder palpable to umbilicus. Right flank tenderness on percussion (CVA). Dry mucous membranes. Cool, mottled extremities.
hints.img: Renal ultrasound (STAT): Bilateral hydronephrosis grade 3. Bladder volume estimated 900mL — markedly distended. Post-catheter drainage: 850mL foul-smelling turbid urine. Right kidney: Perinephric stranding. CT Abdomen/Pelvis: Bilateral hydronephrosis. No calculi. Bladder wall thickening. Right ureteral dilation — not obstructed by stone.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 28,400 with 94% neutrophils and 22% bands. Hgb 11.2. Platelets 88,000 (thrombocytopenia — early DIC).
lab_results.CMP: Creatinine 4.8 (baseline estimated 1.1). BUN 92. Na 128. K 5.8. Bicarbonate 14.
lab_results.EKG: Sinus tachycardia 124bpm. Peaked T waves V1-V4.
lab_results.UA: WBCs too numerous to count. Nitrites positive. WBC casts — infection localized to upper tract/kidney. Bacteria on unspun specimen.
lab_results.ABG: Lactate 5.8 mmol/L. pH 7.22. HCO3 12. Septic shock.
lab_results.Specialty: Blood cultures x2: Preliminary Gram-negative rods (E. coli). Urine culture: >100,000 CFU/mL E. coli — ESBL producing. Oxybutynin (anticholinergic) caused urinary retention in setting of functional BPH equivalent — obstruction above which pyelonephritis developed, then seeded bloodstream. Ureteral stenting + IV ertapenem.

hx_brief: 29yo female presents to the ED with the third episode this year of massive facial and tongue swelling with throat tightness. Each episode lasts 3-4 days. Epinephrine and diphenhydramine given every time — minimal response. Allergist found no triggers.

hints.hx: First episode at age 14 — lips swelled to 'sausage size' after a dental procedure. Mother and maternal grandmother have similar episodes. Episodes also involve severe abdominal cramping and vomiting without any skin involvement — these were misdiagnosed as 'appendicitis' twice. She takes lisinopril — started 2 years ago. She stopped it after the first episode this year but episodes continue. No urticaria during any episode. No pruritus. No identifiable trigger.
hints.pe: Massively edematous lips, tongue, and floor of mouth — non-pitting, non-erythematous, non-pruritic. Uvula edematous. Stridor present. Oropharynx: 40% narrowing. No urticaria or wheals anywhere on skin. No angioectasias. Abdomen: diffuse mild tenderness, voluntary guarding — abdominal attack concurrent.
hints.img: Neck CT: Diffuse submucosal edema of the pharynx, tongue base, and supraglottic structures. No abscess. No foreign body. CT abdomen: Diffuse bowel wall edema, ascites — abdominal angioedema attack.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.Specialty: C4 level: 3 mg/dL (critically low — normal 16-38). C4 low even between attacks. C1q: Normal. C3: Normal. C1-inhibitor functional assay: 8% of normal activity (severely deficient). C1-inhibitor antigen level: 12% of normal. Tryptase: Normal — confirming non-mast cell mechanism. C1-INH gene sequencing: frameshift mutation in SERPING1. Diagnosis: Hereditary Angioedema Type I (C1-INH deficiency) — autosomal dominant.

hx_brief: 64yo male with a history of bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome and mild heart failure on furosemide presents with 4 months of progressive exertional dyspnea and bilateral leg swelling that has not responded to diuretics.

hints.hx: Carpal tunnel released bilaterally 2 years ago. Now his fingers are numb again. Notices 'purple bruises' around his eyes that appear after minor trauma or coughing. His tongue feels thick. Unintentional weight loss of 11 pounds. Prior cardiologist called his echo findings 'hypertensive heart disease' — wall thickness 16mm. On furosemide 80mg daily with minimal response. No alcohol. No family history of heart disease.
hints.pe: BP 100/68. HR 92. JVP elevated 14cm with prominent x and y descents. S3 gallop. Bibasilar crackles. Macroglossia — tongue enlarged with lateral indentations from teeth. Periorbital ecchymosis ('raccoon eyes') present spontaneously. Firm, waxy papules on flexor forearms. 3+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities. Peripheral neuropathy — reduced pinprick stocking distribution.
hints.img: Echo: Concentric LV hypertrophy — wall thickness 18mm (severe). 'Sparkling' or granular myocardial echogenicity. Restrictive filling pattern — E/A ratio 3.2, E/e' ratio 24. EF 45% (mildly reduced). Biatrial enlargement. CXR: Cardiomegaly. Bilateral pleural effusions. No pulmonary vascular congestion disproportionate to effusions.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.2 (severely low). Total protein 8.4 (protein gap 6.2). Creatinine 1.9. ALP 240.
lab_results.EKG: Pseudo-infarction pattern: Q waves V1-V3. Low voltage in limb leads — paradoxical given LVH on echo (highly specific for infiltrative cardiomyopathy).
lab_results.UA: Protein 4+ on dipstick. 24hr urine protein 5.8g (nephrotic range). UPEP: Free lambda light chains (Bence Jones).
lab_results.Coags: Factor X activity: 28% (low — amyloid binds and inactivates factor X; specific association).
lab_results.Specialty: Serum free light chains: Lambda 840 mg/L. Kappa 18 mg/L. Kappa/lambda ratio: 0.02 (severely abnormal — lambda predominant). Fat pad biopsy: Congo Red stain — apple-green birefringence under polarized light. Amyloid P scintigraphy: Cardiac and renal uptake. Bone marrow: 18% lambda-restricted plasma cells. Diagnosis: AL amyloidosis (lambda light chain type) with cardiac, renal, and peripheral nerve involvement.

hx_brief: 46yo male presents to his PCP with 6 months of episodic severe headaches, flushing, and palpitations. He has been told he has 'migraines with autonomic features' and has tried 3 different preventive medications without relief.

hints.hx: Episodes are unpredictable — some wake him from sleep (migraines don't typically wake patients). BP measured during one ER visit: 230/128 — resolved spontaneously before any treatment was given. Between episodes he feels completely normal. Episodes last 20-40 minutes and leave him exhausted. No preceding visual aura. Mother and maternal uncle both had 'endocrine tumors.' He mentions mild tremor and has lost 8 pounds over 6 months without trying.
hints.pe: Between episodes: BP 148/94. HR 84. Mild diaphoresis. No tremor currently. No thyroid enlargement. No cafe-au-lait spots. No neurofibromas. No Cushingoid features. Abdomen: no bruit, no palpable mass.
hints.img: MRI Abdomen with gadolinium: 4.2cm right adrenal mass — heterogeneous with central necrosis, characteristic 'salt and pepper' appearance on T2. Adrenal vein sampling not indicated — biochemical confirmation first. 123I-MIBG scintigraphy: Intense uptake in right adrenal mass. No extra-adrenal uptake.

lab_results.CMP: Glucose 192 (stress hyperglycemia from catecholamine excess). Otherwise normal.
lab_results.EKG: Sinus tachycardia during episode. Occasional PVCs. No ischemic changes.
lab_results.Specialty: Plasma free metanephrines: Normetanephrine 12.8x ULN. Metanephrine 6.4x ULN. 24hr urine catecholamines: Norepinephrine 6,800 µg/day (markedly elevated). Epinephrine 280 µg/day (elevated). VMA 58mg/day (elevated). Genetic panel: RET proto-oncogene — pathogenic variant (Cys634Arg) — MEN2A. Calcitonin elevated — medullary thyroid carcinoma screening positive. Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) must precede beta-blockade before surgical resection.

hx_brief: 53yo male referred to gastroenterology for 'iron deficiency anemia that won't respond to supplementation.' His PCP treated him empirically with IV iron twice in one year.

hints.hx: Iron deficiency confirmed by labs but source never identified. No obvious GI bleeding — no melena, no hematochezia, no hematemesis. Colonoscopy 8 months ago: normal. Upper endoscopy 6 months ago: 'mild gastritis.' Lost 14 pounds over 8 months despite good appetite initially. Now noticing mild dysphagia with solids for the past 6 weeks. Denies heartburn. 35 pack-year smoking history. Drinks 2-3 scotches nightly. No family GI history.
hints.pe: Cachectic. BMI 18.4. Temporal wasting. Left supraclavicular lymph node — 1.2cm, firm, non-tender (Virchow's node). Mild hoarseness. No hepatomegaly.
hints.img: Repeat upper endoscopy: Prior scope likely missed — new view of mid-esophagus at 28cm reveals a friable circumferential mucosal lesion 3.1cm with irregular borders. CT Chest/Abdomen: Circumferential mid-esophageal wall thickening. Multiple enlarged mediastinal and celiac lymph nodes. No liver metastases. PET: FDG-avid primary and nodal disease — no distant metastases confirmed.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.0 (severely low). Ca 11.8 (elevated — PTHrP). CEA 18.4 (elevated). SCC antigen elevated.
lab_results.Specialty: Biopsy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma — poorly differentiated. p40 positive. CK5/6 positive. TTF-1 negative. PD-L1 CPS 18. Staging cT3N2M0. H. pylori negative. Note: Upper mid-esophageal location, smoking + alcohol history, and elevated SCC antigen — classic for squamous cell. The prior 'normal' upper endoscopy likely did not reach the mid-esophagus adequately. Iron deficiency from occult blood loss — the presenting complaint.

hx_brief: 42yo female with known type 1 diabetes and celiac disease presents with 3 months of profound fatigue, hypotension, and weight loss. Her endocrinologist increased her insulin dose twice without improvement.

hints.hx: Requires less insulin than ever despite unchanged diet — hypoglycemia is becoming more frequent and severe. Fatigue is profound — cannot work full days. Orthostatic — nearly faints when standing. Lost 12 pounds. Salt craving — actively adds salt to everything. Increased skin pigmentation — her spouse noticed it first. Has had autoimmune thyroid disease and celiac — both well controlled.
hints.pe: BP sitting 94/58, standing 72/44 (significant orthostasis). HR 108. Diffuse hyperpigmentation — especially notable in palmar creases, buccal mucosa, and a surgical scar from appendectomy (scars hyperpigment in primary adrenal insufficiency). No Cushingoid features. Axillary and pubic hair loss.
hints.img: CT Abdomen: Bilateral adrenal glands small and atrophic — no hemorrhage, no mass, no calcification. No lymphadenopathy. CXR: Small heart size (hypoadrenalism). MRI Pituitary: Normal — confirms primary, not secondary adrenal insufficiency.

lab_results.CBC: Mild normocytic anemia Hgb 10.4. Mild eosinophilia 8%.
lab_results.CMP: Na 126 (hyponatremia). K 5.8 (hyperkalemia). Glucose 62 (hypoglycemia). BUN/Cr ratio elevated — prerenal.
lab_results.EKG: Sinus tachycardia. Peaked T waves from hyperkalemia.
lab_results.UA: Low specific gravity — inability to concentrate urine.
lab_results.ABG: Mild non-anion gap metabolic acidosis — hyperchloremic from aldosterone deficiency.
lab_results.Specialty: Basal cortisol AM: 2.1 µg/dL (severely low; normal >18). ACTH stimulation test (250µg cosyntropin): Peak cortisol at 60min: 4.8 µg/dL (normal response >18). ACTH level: 2,480 pg/mL (markedly elevated — primary adrenal failure). 21-hydroxylase antibodies: Positive — autoimmune (Addison's disease). DHEAS: Undetectable. Aldosterone: Undetectable. Renin: Markedly elevated. Confirms autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease).

hx_brief: 44yo male presents with 6 months of bilateral calf pain, testicular pain, and a new skin ulcer on his right ankle. His PCP treated him for peripheral artery disease — ABI normal. Rheumatology referral for 'unclear vasculitis.'

hints.hx: Calf pain is worse with walking but ABI is normal — confusing. Testicular pain is bilateral, dull, episodic — urology found no infection, no torsion, no mass. The ankle ulcer appeared 3 months ago after minor trauma and has not healed despite wound care. He had an episode of gross hematuria 2 months ago — cystoscopy: normal bladder, ureteral hemorrhage. He has hypertension for 3 years. No diabetes. He has had a 15-pound weight loss. He was tested for hepatitis B — positive HBsAg.
hints.pe: BP 168/104 right arm, 142/88 left arm. Livedo reticularis on bilateral lower extremities. Right ankle: 2.5cm punched-out ulcer with surrounding erythema. Bilateral testicular tenderness on palpation — no masses. Absent right dorsalis pedis pulse. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness. No rashes elsewhere.
hints.img: CT angiography abdomen/pelvis: Multiple microaneurysms (2-5mm) at branch points of renal, hepatic, and mesenteric arteries — classic beaded appearance. No involvement of aorta. No glomerular vessels involved. Renal biopsy: Necrotizing arteritis of medium-sized vessels — fibrinoid necrosis of the media. No immune complex deposition. No glomerulonephritis.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 13,200. Eosinophilia absent. Hgb 10.4 (anemia of chronic inflammation).
lab_results.CMP: Cr 2.1 (renal involvement). BUN 34. Elevated ESR 88, CRP 64.
lab_results.UA: 3+ blood, 2+ protein — renal arteritis with infarction, not glomerulonephritis.
lab_results.Specialty: Mesenteric angiography: Multiple saccular microaneurysms at vessel bifurcations throughout the celiac, SMA, and renal arterial beds. Classic 'string of beads' pattern. ANCA: negative (p-ANCA and c-ANCA). HBsAg positive — HBV-associated PAN. Hepatitis B DNA: 2.4 million IU/mL. Diagnosis: Polyarteritis Nodosa (HBV-associated).

hx_brief: 31yo female with a 12-year history of IBS and refractory iron deficiency anemia referred to hematology after a bone marrow biopsy came back 'normal.' She has failed oral iron 3 times.

hints.hx: Iron deficiency anemia documented since age 19 — never adequately explained. Three courses of oral iron failed due to GI intolerance; IV iron two years ago — hemoglobin improved then dropped again. 'IBS' symptoms: bloating, alternating diarrhea and constipation, worsened after bread and pasta. Fracture of the second metatarsal on a routine jog at age 28 — stress fracture from osteoporosis. Sister has type 1 diabetes. Mother has thyroid disease. Rash on elbows — biopsied as 'eczema' at age 24.
hints.pe: BMI 17.8 — underweight despite good appetite. Pallor. Dental enamel erosions — from acid regurgitation of poorly digested food. Angular cheilitis. Abdominal distension, mild diffuse tenderness. Excoriated papulovesicular rash on bilateral elbows and knees.
hints.img: Capsule endoscopy: Scalloping and flattening of small bowel villi in duodenum and proximal jejunum. Mosaic pattern. Loss of Kerckring's folds. DXA scan: L-spine T-score -2.8 (osteoporosis). Forearm T-score -2.4.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 7.2 — microcytic (MCV 64). Ferritin 3. Folate deficient. B12 borderline low.
lab_results.CMP: Albumin 2.3. Calcium 7.0 (hypocalcemia — malabsorption). Magnesium 1.1 (low). ALP 210 (elevated — bone turnover from vitamin D deficiency). Vitamin D 8 ng/mL (severely deficient).
lab_results.Specialty: IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG): 218 U/mL (strongly positive; normal <4). Total IgA: Normal (ruling out IgA deficiency as false-negative cause). Skin biopsy (elbow): Direct immunofluorescence — granular IgA deposits at dermal-epidermal junction (dermatitis herpetiformis — pathognomonic for celiac disease). Duodenal biopsy: Marsh 3c — total villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia. Intraepithelial lymphocytosis.

hx_brief: 68yo male with CKD stage 3 and hypertension presents to his nephrologist's office for a routine follow-up. His creatinine, previously stable at 1.8 for years, has risen to 3.4 over the past 3 weeks.

hints.hx: Was started on lisinopril 5 months ago (was already on furosemide). His orthopedic surgeon added celecoxib 4 weeks ago for knee osteoarthritis, and he's been taking it daily. No symptoms of urinary obstruction. No recent contrast studies. No new infections. No NSAIDs before celecoxib. Drinking normally. BP was actually better controlled recently.
hints.pe: BP 82/50 (baseline 138/86). HR 116. Confused. Dry mucous membranes. JVP not elevated — volume depleted. No rales. No peripheral edema. Abdomen: soft. No CVA tenderness. No bladder distension.
hints.img: Renal ultrasound: Kidneys 9.2cm bilaterally (stable). No hydronephrosis. No renal artery stenosis on Doppler. No stones. Bladder: empty after voiding, post-void residual 20mL (normal).

lab_results.CMP: Creatinine 3.4 (up from baseline 1.8 over 3 weeks). BUN 88. BUN:Cr ratio 26 (>20 — prerenal). Potassium 6.4 (dangerous). Bicarbonate 16. Na 132.
lab_results.EKG: Peaked T waves V2-V4. Widened QRS 114ms. Sinus tachycardia — hyperkalemia emergency.
lab_results.UA: Hyaline casts only. No granular casts. No RBC casts. Urine osmolality 640 mOsm/kg (concentrating — tubules intact).
lab_results.Specialty: Spot urine sodium: 4 mEq/L. FeNa: 0.2% (confirms prerenal — tubular reabsorption intact). FEUrea: 24% (also prerenal). Mechanism: COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) — same mechanism as non-selective NSAIDs — blocks prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation. In combination with ACE inhibitor (efferent dilation blocked) and diuretic (volume depletion) = 'triple whammy' nephrotoxicity. Celecoxib NOT safer than non-selective NSAIDs in CKD patients on ACEi/diuretics.

hx_brief: 19yo male college freshman presents to student health with 2 days of dysuria and urethral discharge. He was given trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole empirically 3 days ago by a walk-in clinic without cultures sent. No improvement.

hints.hx: Unprotected sexual intercourse with a new partner 2 weeks ago. Whitish-yellow urethral discharge, worse in the morning. Dysuria significant. No testicular pain. No anal symptoms. No prior STIs. His partner has not been tested. He mentions one episode of mild left knee swelling that resolved on its own 5 days ago — he attributed it to 'sleeping funny.'
hints.pe: Temperature 37.4C. Whitish-yellow mucopurulent discharge expressible from urethral meatus. No scrotal tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Left knee: normal on exam today. Skin: two small tender papular lesions on right palm — he hadn't noticed them.
hints.img: Urethral swab collected. Rectal swab collected (he denies receptive anal intercourse but reports it was 'possible'). Throat swab collected. Urine NAAT sent. Pelvic ultrasound: Not indicated — no abdominal or pelvic symptoms.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.UA: 5-10 WBCs/hpf. No nitrites (gram-negatives like gonorrhea don't always cause nitrite-positive UA). Gram stain of urethral discharge: Gram-negative diplococci within PMNs — diagnostic.
lab_results.Specialty: Urine NAAT: Neisseria gonorrhoeae POSITIVE. Chlamydia trachomatis POSITIVE (concurrent). Rectal NAAT: Gonorrhea positive (asymptomatic rectal). Throat NAAT: Gonorrhea positive (asymptomatic pharyngeal). HIV rapid test: Negative. RPR: Negative. Note: TMP-SMX has NO activity against gonorrhea — explains treatment failure. Treatment: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + doxycycline 100mg BID x7 days. Partner notification mandatory. The migratory knee swelling + palm lesions = early disseminated gonococcal infection signs.

hx_brief: 55yo male with a 4-month history of back pain, fatigue, and 3 infections presents to the ED after tripping on a curb caused a spiral femur fracture.

hints.hx: Back pain is constant, worse at night, unresponsive to NSAIDs. Three infections in 4 months: 2 pneumonias (one with unusual organism — Streptococcus pneumoniae serotype resistant to his prior vaccines), 1 sinusitis. New polyuria and polydipsia. Lost 19 pounds. No lymphadenopathy noticed. He assumed the fracture was because he's 'getting old.'
hints.pe: Pale, cachectic. Midline thoracic spinal tenderness T9-T12. No focal neurologic deficits. No lymphadenopathy. Sternal tenderness on direct palpation. No hepatosplenomegaly. Pathological femur fracture — crepitus on passive motion.
hints.img: Skeletal survey: Lytic punched-out lesions — skull (multiple), vertebrae (T9, T11, L2), ribs, and pelvis. No sclerotic lesions (distinguishes from prostate cancer metastases). PET-CT: FDG-avid lesions throughout axial skeleton and femur. MRI spine: Epidural soft tissue at T11 approaching thecal sac — pre-cord compression.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 7.0 — normocytic. WBC 3.8. Platelets 102,000. Peripheral smear: Rouleaux formation (RBCs stacking like coins).
lab_results.CMP: Calcium 14.6 (markedly elevated). Creatinine 3.4. Total protein 11.8. Albumin 2.4. Globulin gap 9.4. LDH 680.
lab_results.UA: Protein 4+ by dipstick. SSA test: Negative for albumin (Bence Jones proteins — light chains — not detected by dipstick sulfosalicylic acid precipitation — a classic trap).
lab_results.Specialty: SPEP: IgG kappa M-spike 5.2 g/dL. UPEP: Bence Jones proteinuria — free kappa light chains. Beta-2 microglobulin: 9.2 mg/L (markedly elevated — adverse prognosis). Bone marrow biopsy: 52% clonal plasma cells. FISH: t(4;14) and del(17p) — double-hit high-risk cytogenetics. R-ISS Stage III. CRAB criteria all present: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions.

hx_brief: 41yo female with 3 months of intermittent double vision and progressive difficulty with prolonged speaking presents to neurology. She was told she had 'anxiety' at an urgent care visit 6 weeks ago.

hints.hx: Double vision first appeared while driving — horizontal diplopia that worsened late in the day. Speech becomes slurred after talking for more than 10 minutes — 'jaw gets tired.' Cannot finish a full restaurant meal — jaw fatigues with chewing. Morning symptoms: normal. Afternoon/evening: significantly impaired. Brief rest reliably improves all symptoms. No sensory symptoms. No family history.
hints.pe: 8am exam: Near-normal strength. Mild ptosis right eye only. 3pm exam (after 30 minutes of sustained activity): Bilateral ptosis. Diplopia on sustained lateral gaze. Proximal arm weakness — cannot sustain arms overhead. Ice pack test: Placing ice over right eye for 2 minutes reduces ptosis by 4mm (cooling improves ACh release). Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3Hz: 18% amplitude decrement — abnormal (>10% is significant).
hints.img: CT Chest with contrast: 3.8cm heterogeneous anterior mediastinal mass — thymoma versus thymic carcinoma. PET: FDG-avid anterior mediastinal mass — no distant uptake.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-AChR binding antibodies: 32.4 nmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <0.4 nmol/L). Anti-striated muscle antibodies: Positive — associated with thymoma. Anti-MuSK: Negative (if AChR negative, MuSK would be tested). Single-fiber EMG: Increased jitter (>55µs mean consecutive difference) — most sensitive test for NMJ dysfunction. Edrophonium (Tensilon) test: Positive — transient improvement in ptosis. CT-guided thymoma biopsy: WHO type B2 thymoma. Thymectomy planned + pyridostigmine initiated.

hx_brief: 72yo male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the ED 90 minutes after sudden onset of left arm weakness and difficulty finding words. His wife called 911 immediately.

hints.hx: Known PAF — on aspirin only because 'his cardiologist said his risk was low' (CHA2DS2-VASc is actually 4). HTN, type 2 diabetes. Weakness came on over 30 seconds — fully abrupt. Expressive aphasia — knows what he wants to say but words won't come. Never had a TIA. Last seen normal 95 minutes ago.
hints.pe: BP 206/112. HR 88 irregularly irregular. NIHSS 16. Right facial droop (upper motor neuron pattern). Right arm strength 1/5. Right leg 3/5. Expressive aphasia — follows simple commands. Left gaze preference. No ataxia. No sensory deficit.
hints.img: CT Head (non-contrast, 15min): No hemorrhage. Subtle loss of gray-white differentiation left MCA territory — early ischemic change. ASPECTS score 7. CTA Head/Neck: Complete occlusion left MCA M1 segment. No significant extracranial carotid stenosis. CT Perfusion: Large penumbra (salvageable tissue) in left MCA territory — core infarct 18mL, penumbra 88mL.

lab_results.CMP: Glucose 178. Creatinine 1.2.
lab_results.EKG: Atrial fibrillation — irregularly irregular, no P waves, rate 88bpm.
lab_results.Specialty: IV tPA administered (within 4.5hr window — last known normal 95min ago). Mechanical thrombectomy performed — TICI 2b reperfusion achieved. TEE performed post-procedure: Dense spontaneous echo contrast ('smoke') in left atrium. Thrombus in left atrial appendage — confirmed cardioembolic source. CHA2DS2-VASc recalculated: Stroke+1, HTN+1, DM+1, age>65+1 = 4. Annual stroke risk 4% — anticoagulation was strongly indicated. Aspirin alone is insufficient for CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 in men.

hx_brief: 26yo male presents for evaluation of persistent bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy for 4 weeks. His PCP treated him for infectious mononucleosis — monospot negative — and then for streptococcal pharyngitis — throat culture negative.

hints.hx: Lymphadenopathy is painless and has been enlarging slowly. Drenching night sweats requiring change of sheets. Lost 12 pounds in 6 weeks without trying. Whole-body pruritus without visible rash — he has scratch marks on his trunk and arms. One very specific symptom: severe right neck pain and flushing lasting 20 minutes after having 2 beers at a party — never experienced this before.
hints.pe: Firm, rubbery, non-tender lymph nodes — bilateral cervical (largest 3.4cm), bilateral supraclavicular, bilateral axillary. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Spleen palpable 5cm below left costal margin. Liver non-palpable. No skin lesions. No tonsillar enlargement.
hints.img: PET/CT: FDG-avid lymphadenopathy — bilateral cervical, mediastinal, bilateral hilar, retroperitoneal. Splenomegaly with splenic FDG uptake. No bone marrow involvement on PET. Ann Arbor Stage IIIB (B symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%).

lab_results.CBC: WBC 15,200 with eosinophilia 16% (absolute eosinophilia — paraneoplastic). Hgb 10.4. Platelets 480,000 (reactive thrombocytosis).
lab_results.CMP: LDH 580 (elevated). Albumin 2.6. ESR 92mm/hr.
lab_results.Specialty: Excisional biopsy (right cervical node): Reed-Sternberg cells — large binucleated cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli surrounded by lymphocytes. Immunohistochemistry: CD15+, CD30+, CD20-, CD45-. EBER in-situ hybridization: Positive (EBV-driven). Subtype: Mixed cellularity classical Hodgkin lymphoma. Alcohol-induced lymph node pain: pathognomonic for Hodgkin lymphoma — mechanism unclear but highly specific. Bone marrow biopsy: Negative. PET-based staging: Stage IIIB. ABVD chemotherapy planned.

hx_brief: 80yo female brought in by her son after she left the stove on three times in the past month and missed a doctor's appointment she had marked on her calendar. 'She was always so sharp.'

hints.hx: Memory decline insidious over 3 years — son initially thought it was 'normal aging.' Now she cannot recall what she ate for breakfast, gets confused about the day of the week, and recently accused her cleaning lady of stealing jewelry later found in a shoebox. Still lives alone. No focal weakness, no gait disturbance, no incontinence. She passed a 'dementia screening' at her PCP 18 months ago (MMSE 26/30 at that time — missed early impairment).
hints.pe: MoCA: 12/30 — impaired immediate recall (1/5), delayed recall (0/5), orientation (4/6), visuospatial (1/4). MMSE 17/30. No focal motor deficits. Normal gait and coordination. No tremor. No rigidity. Frontal release signs: Snout reflex present.
hints.img: MRI Brain: Bilateral hippocampal volume loss disproportionate to age — medial temporal lobe atrophy. Entorhinal cortex thinning. Widened temporal horns. No strategic infarcts. No mass lesion. No hydrocephalus. Amyloid PET (florbetapir): Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition — frontal, parietal, temporal, and hippocampal regions.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: CSF biomarkers: Aβ42: 248 pg/mL (severely low — amyloid deposits in brain, less in CSF). Phospho-tau 181: 112 pg/mL (markedly elevated — neuronal injury). Total tau: 680 pg/mL (elevated). Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio: 0.04 (abnormal <0.10). Aβ42/p-tau ratio: 2.2 (abnormal — confirms AD pathology). ApoE genotyping: ε4/ε4 homozygous — highest genetic risk for late-onset AD (15x population risk). Lewy body panel negative. RPR negative. TSH normal. B12 normal. Confirms Alzheimer's disease — amyloid and tau biomarker criteria met.

hx_brief: 26yo male presents to neurology with 4 months of progressive bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, new cognitive difficulties, and two episodes of branch retinal artery occlusion in different eyes. Extensive stroke workup: negative.

hints.hx: Hearing loss started in the right ear, then the left — audiologist confirmed bilateral sensorineural pattern, primarily low and mid frequencies. Cognitive changes: memory lapses, word-finding difficulty, personality change — his girlfriend says 'he's not himself.' Two separate branch retinal artery occlusions — right eye 4 months ago, left eye 6 weeks ago — ophthalmology found no embolic source, no coagulopathy, no vasculitis on standard workup. No headache. No fever. No rash. He is otherwise healthy, non-smoker, no drugs.
hints.pe: Alert but slowed processing. MMSE 23/30 — loses points on recall and attention. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss confirmed. HEENT: no oral ulcers, no rash. Fundoscopy: branch arterial whitening left eye — Gass plaques (yellowish arterial wall deposits at branching points). Neurologic: no focal motor or sensory deficits. No meningismus.
hints.img: MRI brain: Multiple small DWI-restricted lesions in the white matter — NOT following vascular territories, NOT in watershed distribution. Corpus callosum involvement — 'snowball lesions' on sagittal FLAIR. No enhancement. MRA: normal large vessels. LP: mildly elevated protein 68, lymphocytes 22 — mild CSF inflammation.

lab_results.CMP: Normal.
lab_results.Coags: Normal. Factor V Leiden negative. Antiphospholipid antibodies negative. Protein C/S normal.
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-endothelial cell antibodies (AECA): markedly positive. Anti-alpha-SNAP antibodies: positive — research assay confirming Susac syndrome endotheliopathy. Fluorescein angiography: branch retinal artery occlusions with Gass plaques at bifurcation points — pathognomonic. No infectious cause identified (HSV, VZV, CMV, HIV, syphilis all negative). Cochlear MRI: bilateral cochlear enhancement on gadolinium. Diagnosis: Susac Syndrome (retinocochleocerebral vasculopathy).

hx_brief: 63yo male with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) presents to the ED with worsening dyspnea over 3 days. He has been 'feeling better' and stopped his diuretics 3 weeks ago to 'take a break.'

hints.hx: Prior MI 6 years ago. On carvedilol, lisinopril, spironolactone, furosemide (self-discontinued). Has a CRT-D device implanted 2 years ago. Now sleeps in recliner. Cannot walk to the bathroom without stopping. Gained 14 pounds in 3 weeks. Wakes at 4am gasping. Coughs when lying flat.
hints.pe: BP 88/60 (baseline 110/70). HR 104 — irregularly irregular (atrial fibrillation). JVP 18cm at 45 degrees. Bilateral crackles to scapulae. S3 gallop at apex. Hepatojugular reflux positive. Hepatomegaly 4cm below right costal margin — tender. Ascites — shifting dullness. 4+ pitting edema to thighs. Extremities cool with prolonged cap refill.
hints.img: CXR: Gross cardiomegaly. Bilateral pleural effusions. Vascular redistribution. Kerley B lines. Echo: EF 18% (reduced from 25% baseline — acute on chronic). Severe functional MR. Elevated estimated PCWP >25mmHg. LVEDP 32mmHg on hemodynamic monitoring.

lab_results.CMP: Na 126 (dilutional hyponatremia — poor prognostic sign in CHF). Creatinine 2.8 (cardiorenal syndrome). BUN 72. Albumin 3.0. AST 148, ALT 112 (congestive hepatopathy).
lab_results.EKG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. LBBB (paced rhythm from CRT). QRS 168ms.
lab_results.Trop: 0.22 ng/mL (chronically elevated from myocardial wall stress — not acute MI).
lab_results.ABG: pO2 58 on 6L NC. pH 7.34 — mild respiratory acidosis from pulmonary edema.
lab_results.Specialty: BNP: 6,800 pg/mL (severely elevated). NT-proBNP: >35,000 pg/mL. PA catheter (Swan-Ganz): PCWP 30mmHg (severely elevated). CO 2.8 L/min (reduced). SVR 2,200 dynes (markedly elevated — cardiogenic shock physiology). Diagnosis: Decompensated ischemic cardiomyopathy with low-output state (warm/wet → now cold/wet). Requires IV diuresis + vasodilators; consider inotrope or mechanical circulatory support.

hx_brief: 72yo male with atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents with 4 hours of severe periumbilical pain out of proportion to exam. He had a similar milder episode 3 weeks ago that resolved. His INR today is 3.2.

hints.hx: Pain started suddenly at rest, crampy then became constant. He describes it as the worst abdominal pain of his life — 'like my insides are being squeezed.' Vomited twice. No diarrhea yet — but had bloody bowel movement 20 minutes before arrival. He has known atrial fibrillation, CHF with EF 30%, and peripheral artery disease. He was cardioverted 5 weeks ago. Warfarin started 4 weeks ago. The prior episode 3 weeks ago: 'food pain' — attributed to mesenteric angina.
hints.pe: Diaphoretic, writhing in pain. BP 88/54, HR 112 (irregular), Temp 37.2C. Abdomen: periumbilical tenderness — but soft, no guarding, no rigidity. Bowel sounds hypoactive. Rectal exam: gross blood. The DISCREPANCY: severe pain with relatively benign exam — classic.
hints.img: CT angiography abdomen: Filling defect in the superior mesenteric artery — thrombus at the origin, 60% occlusion. Small bowel: diffuse wall thickening and pneumatosis intestinalis of the mid-jejunum. Mesenteric fat stranding. Portal venous gas — late ominous finding. No free air yet.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 24,600 — leukocytosis from bowel ischemia. Hgb 11.2.
lab_results.CMP: Lactate 7.8 mmol/L — critical. BUN 44, Cr 2.1. Phosphate 6.8 — elevated phosphate from ischemic bowel cell death.
lab_results.EKG: Atrial fibrillation, rate 112. No ischemic changes.
lab_results.Lipase: Normal — critical finding ruling out pancreatitis.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.18, bicarb 10, lactate 7.8 — severe metabolic acidosis from bowel ischemia.
lab_results.Coags: INR 3.2 — anticoagulated, yet embolic event still occurred (AF with low EF — clot despite warfarin).
lab_results.Specialty: Emergent mesenteric angiography: SMA embolus confirmed at the origin of the middle colic artery. Catheter-directed thrombolysis initiated. Repeat imaging 6 hours: partial recanalization — 40% residual stenosis. Exploratory laparotomy: 80cm necrotic mid-jejunum resected. Patient survived with short bowel syndrome.

hx_brief: 36yo female with a 2-year history of intermittent episodes of flushing, wheezing, and explosive diarrhea is referred to gastroenterology after being diagnosed and treated for 'IBS' and 'rosacea' without improvement.

hints.hx: Episodes happen 4-6 times per week. Severe flushing — non-pruritic — starting on face and spreading to chest. Explosive watery diarrhea during or after flushing. Occasional wheezing and chest tightness. Triggered by alcohol, stress, certain foods. Lost 10 pounds over 2 years. Denied a palpable abdominal mass at first, but now admits to a 'lump' in her right lower abdomen she assumed was a bowel loop.
hints.pe: During an episode: BP 168/98. Facial and truncal flushing — non-urticarial. Bilateral fine crackles (bronchoconstriction). Right-sided abdominal mass 4cm, firm, non-tender in RLQ. Cardiac auscultation: systolic murmur at LLSB and mid-systolic click — tricuspid and pulmonic valve involvement.
hints.img: CT Abdomen: 2.8cm ileal mass with 'desmoplastic reaction' — spiculated mesenteric mass with tethering of bowel loops (classic for midgut carcinoid). Multiple hepatic lesions 0.5-1.5cm — metastases. Octreoscan: Intense uptake in ileal primary and liver metastases — confirms somatostatin receptor positivity.

lab_results.CMP: ALP 320 (elevated — hepatic metastases). Otherwise normal.
lab_results.Specialty: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA: 94mg/24hr (severely elevated; normal <8mg/24hr). Serum chromogranin A: 1,240 U/L (markedly elevated). Echo: Tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonic stenosis — Hedinger syndrome (carcinoid heart disease). Right-sided valves affected because serotonin is inactivated by pulmonary circulation before reaching left heart. Serotonin is metabolized by the liver — hepatic metastases bypass this, allowing serotonin to reach the systemic circulation and cause carcinoid syndrome. Treatment: Octreotide LAR + hepatic embolization + primary resection if feasible.

hx_brief: 39yo male with progressive bilateral hand weakness and atrophy presents to neurology after being told by three PCPs that he has 'bad carpal tunnel.'

hints.hx: Bilateral hand weakness over 14 months — dropping objects, difficulty with buttons. Both hands affected simultaneously — carpal tunnel is typically unilateral or sequential. Noticed visible 'twitching' in his arms and tongue that he finds disturbing. No sensory symptoms — 'my hands are weak but I can feel everything normally.' Speech has become slightly nasal. Denies swallowing difficulty currently. No family history of neuromuscular disease. No exposure history.
hints.pe: Atrophy of intrinsic hand muscles bilaterally (thenar and hypothenar). Fasciculations: bilateral thighs, bilateral calves, tongue. Grip strength 2/5 bilaterally. Hyperreflexia — brisk biceps, triceps, knee, and ankle reflexes (UMN). Bilateral Babinski signs positive (UMN). Dysarthria — nasal quality. Sensation: Intact to all modalities in all extremities — critical negative.
hints.img: MRI Brain and Spine: Corticospinal tract T2 hyperintensity bilateral internal capsules and along corticospinal tracts — UMN degeneration. No compressive lesion. No signal change in cord. EMG/NCS ordered.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.Specialty: EMG: Fibrillations and positive sharp waves (active denervation) in bilateral upper extremities, lower extremities, and paraspinal muscles — 3 body regions. Giant motor unit potentials (chronic reinnervation). Fasciculation potentials throughout. NCS: Normal sensory nerve conduction velocities and amplitudes throughout — sensory sparing is pathognomonic for ALS (sensory roots are not involved). Meets Awaji-Shima criteria for definite ALS. SOD1 genetic panel: Negative (sporadic ALS). Riluzole initiated. FVC 78% — serial monitoring.

hx_brief: 28yo female referred to rheumatology after 8 months of progressive proximal limb weakness, a 'weird rash' on her knuckles, and a new cough. She was initially evaluated for polymyalgia rheumatica.

hints.hx: Weakness began insidiously — difficulty rising from the floor, climbing stairs, reaching overhead. No muscle pain (polymyalgia rheumatica causes pain, not weakness — wrong diagnosis). The knuckle rash has been present for 4 months, treated as eczema without improvement. New dry cough for 3 weeks — she assumed it was from wildfire smoke. No joint swelling. No Raynaud's. No prior autoimmune history.
hints.pe: Scaly erythematous papules over MCP and PIP joint extensor surfaces bilaterally (Gottron papules — pathognomonic). Heliotrope discoloration bilateral eyelids. V-sign erythema (anterior chest in V-neckline distribution). Mechanic's hands (hyperkeratotic cracking of palmar and lateral finger skin). Proximal upper and lower extremity strength 2+/5. Fine bibasilar crackles (ILD).
hints.img: MRI thighs: Diffuse muscle edema, T2 hyperintensity, enhancement in bilateral quadriceps and hamstrings — active inflammatory myopathy. HRCT Chest: Bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidation lower lobes — organizing pneumonia pattern (ILD).

lab_results.CMP: CK: 8,200 U/L (markedly elevated). AST 320, ALT 280 (muscle origin — not hepatic; LDH elevated confirms). Aldolase: 42 U/L (elevated — myopathy).
lab_results.UA: Myoglobinuria — blood 3+ on dipstick with no RBCs on microscopy.
lab_results.ABG: pO2 70 on room air — ILD causing hypoxemia.
lab_results.Specialty: Anti-Jo-1 antibody: Positive (antisynthetase antibody — associated with ILD, mechanic's hands, arthritis, Raynaud's + myositis = antisynthetase syndrome). Anti-Mi-2: Negative. Anti-MDA5: Negative. Anti-SRP: Negative. ANA: Positive 1:320 speckled. Muscle biopsy: Perifascicular atrophy and necrosis — perifascicular pattern pathognomonic for dermatomyositis (distinguishes from polymyositis and IBM). EMG: Myopathic — short-duration low-amplitude polyphasic MUPs. CT abdomen/pelvis: No occult malignancy (malignancy screening required in DM >40yo).

hx_brief: 34yo male with type 2 diabetes presents with 48 hours of worsening right eye pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. He was prescribed antibiotic eye drops at urgent care yesterday without improvement.

hints.hx: Wears monthly extended-wear soft contact lenses — has been wearing the same pair for 6 weeks without removing them. No prior eye problems. Pain is 9/10 — severe, far beyond typical conjunctivitis. The antibiotic drops (erythromycin) haven't touched the pain. Photophobia causes blepharospasm when examining. No recent corneal trauma. Denies swimming in contact lenses.
hints.pe: Right eye: periorbital edema. Marked conjunctival injection with pronounced limbal (ciliary) flush. Blepharospasm from photophobia. Fluorescein staining: large central corneal ulcer with feathery irregular edges — and a satellite lesion 2mm from the main ulcer. Hypopyon: 2mm white layering in anterior chamber. IOP 32mmHg (elevated from inflammation).
hints.img: Slit-lamp (slit-beam examination): Dense grayish-white infiltrate in anterior stroma. 8mm epithelial defect. Satellite infiltrate adjacent to main lesion — raises concern for atypical organism (Acanthamoeba or fungus in addition to bacteria). Ring infiltrate pattern not present.

lab_results.CMP: Glucose 242 (poorly controlled diabetes — impairs corneal healing and immune response).
lab_results.Specialty: Corneal scraping: Gram stain — numerous Gram-negative rods. Culture (blood agar, chocolate agar, Sabouraud): Pseudomonas aeruginosa — heavy growth. Meropenem-resistant; ciprofloxacin and tobramycin sensitive. Acanthamoeba PCR and culture: Negative. Fungal culture: Negative at 5 days. Corneal confocal microscopy: No Acanthamoeba cysts. Treatment: Fortified topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% q1hr (first 24hrs) + fortified tobramycin. Extended-wear lenses are highest risk — overnight wear increases Pseudomonas risk 10-15 fold.

hx_brief: 57yo male presents to his PCP with 4 months of early satiety, unintentional weight loss of 9 pounds, and fatigue. He is certain it is 'just stress' from his new job.

hints.hx: Cannot finish a full meal — feels full after a few bites. Left-sided abdominal fullness and discomfort when bending over. Mild left shoulder pain — he mentions it dismissively. Night sweats for 3 months. WBC from a pre-employment physical 6 months ago was 22,000 — 'flagged as abnormal, recommended repeat in 3 months' — he never followed up.
hints.pe: Mildly pale. Massive splenomegaly — palpable 16cm below left costal margin, crosses midline. Mild hepatomegaly. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. No sternal tenderness. The left shoulder pain is referred — splenic capsule distension.
hints.img: CT Abdomen/Pelvis: Spleen 26cm longitudinal dimension — massive. No significant lymphadenopathy. Mild hepatomegaly. Splenic infarcts visible (small wedge-shaped areas of hypoperfusion — from vascular occlusion by leukocytes).

lab_results.CBC: WBC 284,000 — full myeloid spectrum: neutrophils, bands, metamyelocytes, myelocytes, promyelocytes, blasts <5%, basophilia 12% (basophilia is characteristic). Hgb 9.4. Platelets 980,000 (thrombocytosis).
lab_results.CMP: Uric acid 11.8 (hyperuricemia from high cell turnover). LDH 2,100 (elevated). B12 markedly elevated >2,000 pg/mL (released from granulocytes — marker of myeloid expansion). Creatinine 1.1.
lab_results.Lipase: Pseudonormally low lipase — WBCs consume lipase (artifact of massive leukocytosis — leukocyte larceny).
lab_results.ABG: Pseudohypoxia if not immediately processed — WBCs consume O2 in vitro.
lab_results.Specialty: BCR-ABL1 quantitative PCR (IS): 94% — markedly positive. Cytogenetics (G-banding): t(9;22)(q34;q11.2) Philadelphia chromosome in 100% of metaphases. FISH: BCR-ABL1 fusion confirmed. LAP (leukocyte alkaline phosphatase) score: 4 (markedly low — differentiates CML from leukemoid reaction where LAP is high). Sokal score: Intermediate risk. Imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) first-line — targets BCR-ABL constitutive kinase activity.

hx_brief: 28yo female presents 8 weeks postpartum with 3 weeks of progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral leg swelling. She was told at her 6-week postpartum visit that 'everything looks good.'

hints.hx: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks. No hypertension, no preeclampsia during pregnancy. No prior cardiac history. Now cannot climb stairs without stopping. Wakes at 2am gasping for air — sleeping propped on 3 pillows. Noticed leg swelling worsening. Attributed dyspnea to 'new mom exhaustion' for 3 weeks before seeking care.
hints.pe: HR 112. BP 92/60. JVP elevated 16cm. Bilateral basal crackles. S3 gallop at apex. Bilateral 3+ pitting edema to mid-thigh. Cool extremities with prolonged cap refill. Hepatojugular reflux positive.
hints.img: Echo: LV severely dilated — LVIDd 74mm (normal <52mm in women). EF 18% — severely reduced (was presumably normal pre-pregnancy). No WMA — global hypokinesis. Moderate functional MR. Biatrial enlargement. Cardiac MRI: Diffuse subendocardial late gadolinium enhancement — inflammatory pattern. No focal fibrosis.

lab_results.CMP: Na 128 (dilutional). Creatinine 1.6. BUN 42.
lab_results.EKG: Sinus tachycardia 112bpm. Diffuse low voltage. Non-specific ST changes. Frequent PVCs.
lab_results.Trop: 0.32 ng/mL (elevated — myocardial injury from inflammatory process).
lab_results.Specialty: BNP: 5,200 pg/mL (severely elevated). NT-proBNP: 28,000 pg/mL. Cardiac MRI: LVEF 18%. Diffuse myocardial edema on T2 mapping — suggests active myocarditis. Prolactin: 92 ng/mL (elevated — still nursing). Anti-cardiac antibody panel: Negative. Viral myocarditis panel: Negative. Endomyocardial biopsy: Mild lymphocytic infiltration — consistent with peripartum cardiomyopathy. Bromocriptine (prolactin inhibitor) added — evidence suggests prolactin 16-kDa cleavage product is pathogenic. Prognosis: ~50% recover EF with treatment.

hx_brief: 49yo male with a BMI of 42 presents to his pulmonologist for the third time this year for 'worsening asthma.' His inhaler use has doubled and he gets winded tying his shoes.

hints.hx: Diagnosed with asthma 4 years ago, but spirometry at that time showed a normal FEV1/FVC ratio — restriction, not obstruction. Multiple failed trials of bronchodilators. Epworth Sleepiness Scale: 18/24 (severely sleepy). Wakes with headaches. Wife reports loud snoring and gasping. Also mentions bilateral ankle swelling and exertional dyspnea. He has been denied bariatric surgery twice pending 'cardiac clearance.'
hints.pe: BMI 42. O2 sat 88% on room air (baseline). Obese neck circumference 48cm. Accessory muscle use at rest. Mild central cyanosis. JVP 12cm — elevated. Loud P2. Right parasternal heave. 2+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Lungs: decreased breath sounds at bases bilaterally — no wheezing.
hints.img: Echo: RV dilation and hypertrophy. Estimated RVSP 68mmHg. Flat interventricular septum (D-sign). TR moderate. EF 55% preserved. LV underfilled. CXR: Cardiomegaly (right-sided). Peripheral pruning of pulmonary vasculature. No infiltrates. CT Chest: No PE. No ILD. Enlarged pulmonary arteries.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 18.4 — polycythemia (secondary, from chronic hypoxemia — EPO-driven).
lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.EKG: Right axis deviation. RVH pattern. P pulmonale. Right bundle branch block.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.34. pO2 52. pCO2 58 (hypercapnia — chronic hypoventilation). HCO3 34 (metabolic compensation). O2 sat 88%.
lab_results.Specialty: Sleep study: AHI 68 events/hour (severe OSA). O2 nadir 68%. Right heart catheterization: mPAP 52mmHg (severely elevated). PCWP 12mmHg (normal — pre-capillary). PVR 8.2 Wood units. CO 3.8 L/min (reduced). Confirms group 3 pulmonary hypertension (from OSA/obesity hypoventilation). CPAP/BiPAP titration + weight loss are primary treatments. Pulmonary vasodilators contraindicated in group 3 PH unless concurrent group 1 PAH confirmed.

hx_brief: 38yo female with known Crohn's disease presents to the ED with severe nausea, vomiting, and profound weakness for 3 days. She has been on long-term prednisone which she abruptly stopped 2 weeks ago when she 'ran out.' BP on arrival: 74/46.

hints.hx: She was on prednisone 15mg daily for 2 years for Crohn's. Ran out of her prescription and could not get a refill 'in time.' Felt fine for 5 days, then progressively worsened: nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, profound fatigue, dizziness on standing. She craves salt — has been eating crackers compulsively. Diarrhea started 2 days ago — she assumed Crohn's flare. She also has hyperpigmentation she attributes to 'tanning easily.' No fever.
hints.pe: Profoundly ill. BP 74/46 supine, undetectable standing. HR 128. Skin: hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, and knuckles — subtle but present. Abdomen: diffuse mild tenderness — no peritoneal signs. No lymphadenopathy. Vitiligo patches on bilateral forearms.
hints.img: CXR: Normal. CT abdomen: Small adrenal glands bilaterally — atrophic from chronic steroid suppression. No adrenal hemorrhage. No adrenal mass. EKG obtained emergently.

lab_results.CBC: WBC 14,200 with eosinophilia (12%) — eosinophilia in adrenal crisis is characteristic. Hgb 13.1.
lab_results.CMP: Na 124 (hyponatremia). K 6.2 (hyperkalemia). Glucose 54 (hypoglycemia). Cr 2.4 (prerenal from hypoperfusion). BUN 52.
lab_results.EKG: Peaked T waves — hyperkalemia. QRS widening 122ms. PR prolongation.
lab_results.ABG: pH 7.22, bicarb 11 — metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion and mineralocorticoid deficiency.
lab_results.Specialty: Random cortisol: 1.2 mcg/dL (critically low — normal stress response >18). ACTH stimulation test: cortisol rises only to 3.4 mcg/dL at 60 minutes (normal >18). ACTH level: undetectable — secondary adrenal insufficiency from HPA axis suppression by chronic exogenous steroids. IV hydrocortisone 100mg given: BP recovered to 106/70 within 90 minutes. Diagnosis: Adrenal Crisis (secondary, from glucocorticoid withdrawal).

hx_brief: 33yo male with 6 months of worsening exertional dyspnea and two episodes of syncope during exercise. He was told 3 years ago he had a 'heart murmur — nothing to worry about.'

hints.hx: Father died suddenly at 38 during a recreational basketball game — 'cardiac arrhythmia.' Two maternal cousins have had unexplained syncope. He has had exertional presyncope for 6 months. Recently stopped playing recreational soccer because the dyspnea was affecting performance. No chest pain. No palpitations. The murmur was heard on a routine physical and dismissed without echo.
hints.pe: BP 124/82. HR 76. Harsh grade 3/6 systolic murmur at left sternal border — increases with standing (reduced preload → increased obstruction → louder murmur) and decreases with squatting (increased preload → decreased obstruction → softer). Sustained apical impulse. S4 gallop. No aortic click. No carotid radiation.
hints.img: Echo: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy — IVS 24mm (posterior wall 10mm). Ratio 2.4. LVOT peak gradient 58mmHg at rest — increases to 94mmHg with Valsalva maneuver. Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of mitral valve. Moderate MR (SAM-related). EF 78% (hyperdynamic). No aortic valve stenosis.

lab_results.CMP: Normal
lab_results.EKG: Voltage criteria for LVH. Deep narrow Q waves in I, aVL, V5-V6 (pseudo-infarction pattern — from septal hypertrophy). ST depression lateral leads.
lab_results.Specialty: Genetic panel: Pathogenic variant in MYBPC3 (cardiac myosin-binding protein C) — c.2905+1G>A splice-site mutation. Autosomal dominant. Father's death now explained. Family cascade screening initiated (cousins and children at 50% risk). Holter monitor: 3 runs of non-sustained VT — elevated SCD risk. ICD implanted. Metoprolol succinate initiated (negative inotrope reduces LVOT gradient). Sports restriction mandatory. Alcohol-septal ablation or surgical myectomy planned.

hx_brief: 52yo male presents with 3 years of weight loss, chronic diarrhea, migratory joint pain, and intermittent fever. He has seen 6 physicians. He was diagnosed with seronegative RA and treated with methotrexate — no improvement.

hints.hx: Weight loss is 40 pounds over 3 years — dramatic. Diarrhea is steatorrhea — greasy, foul-smelling, floats, 4-6 times daily. Joint pain migrates: knees, ankles, wrists — never symmetric for long, never erosive on X-ray despite 'RA' diagnosis. He has had 4 episodes of fever with no identifiable source. He is a farmer, spends time outdoors. Neurological symptoms starting: memory problems, difficulty with eye movements — 'can't look down easily.' His wife notes personality changes over 6 months.
hints.pe: Cachectic. Hyperpigmented skin — diffuse, not sun-exposed areas. Lymphadenopathy: mesenteric (on exam, a mass is palpable in the right lower quadrant). Ophthalmoplegia: limited vertical gaze — can not look downward voluntarily. Nystagmus. Abdomen: dull to percussion in the flanks — ascites. No hepatosplenomegaly.
hints.img: CT abdomen: Massive mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with low-density cores. Ascites. Small bowel wall thickening. MRI brain: signal abnormality in hypothalamus and brainstem. PET: hypermetabolic mesenteric nodes. Small bowel capsule endoscopy: diffuse white shaggy villi throughout jejunum.

lab_results.CMP: Albumin 1.6 (severe malnutrition). Low cholesterol (fat malabsorption). Normal LFTs.
lab_results.Specialty: Small bowel biopsy with PAS stain: Lamina propria packed with foamy macrophages containing PAS-positive, diastase-resistant granules — pathognomonic. Villous blunting with lymphatic dilation. PCR of duodenal biopsy: Tropheryma whipplei DNA confirmed. CSF PCR: Tropheryma whipplei positive — CNS involvement. Diagnosis: Whipple's Disease with CNS involvement. Treatment: ceftriaxone IV x2 weeks → trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole x1-2 years.

hx_brief: 44yo female with 10 months of joint swelling and morning stiffness is referred to rheumatology. Her PCP has been treating her for 'reactive arthritis' with NSAIDs and physical therapy.

hints.hx: Morning stiffness lasting 2-3 hours — she cannot close her hands into a fist until midmorning. Bilateral wrist, MCP, and PIP swelling — symmetric. Fatigue profound. Noticed a small firm nodule on her right elbow 2 months ago. No psoriatic skin or nail changes. No uveitis. No preceding infection. No DIP or spine involvement. No photosensitivity. Smokes 10 cigarettes/day — identified risk factor. Mother and maternal grandmother had similar joint disease.
hints.pe: Symmetric synovitis — bilateral MCPs (2nd-5th), PIPs, and wrists bilaterally. Early ulnar deviation of right hand. DIP joints: normal — key distinguishing feature from osteoarthritis and psoriatic arthritis. Rheumatoid nodule on right olecranon — firm, non-tender. No skin rash. No iritis. No lymphadenopathy.
hints.img: X-ray bilateral hands and wrists: Periarticular osteopenia at MCPs and wrists (early). Marginal erosions at 2nd and 3rd MCP heads bilaterally. No joint space narrowing yet — early structural damage. Musculoskeletal ultrasound: Power Doppler signal (blood flow) within synovium bilaterally — active synovitis confirmed. Synovial hypertrophy grade 2.

lab_results.CBC: Hgb 10.6 — normocytic anemia of chronic disease. Thrombocytosis 560,000 (reactive).
lab_results.CMP: Normal
